page.title: About the Calculators
page.subtitle: Learn about each health metric, the formula behind it, and how to interpret your results.

metadata.title: BMI Calculator — Body Mass Index | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate your Body Mass Index (BMI) using the WHO standard formula. Understand your weight category and associated health risks based on international guidelines.

items.title: BMI Calculator
items.tagline: Are you at a healthy weight for your height?
items.shortDesc: Check if your weight is in a healthy range using the WHO international standard classification.
items.importance: BMI is the most widely used screening tool by doctors, dietitians, and public health professionals worldwide. As of 2022, WHO data shows 2.5 billion adults (43%) are overweight and 890 million (16%) are living with obesity — numbers that have more than doubled since 1990. Knowing your BMI is the first step in identifying potential weight-related health risks such as type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease.
items.description: Body Mass Index (BMI) is a screening measure that estimates whether a person has a healthy body weight relative to their height. The formula was standardised by WHO in 1997 and remains the international benchmark for population-level weight assessment. It is a screening tool — not a diagnostic one — and should always be considered alongside other clinical measures.
items.formula:
Metric:   BMI = weight (kg) ÷ height² (m)
Imperial: BMI = [weight (lbs) ÷ height² (in)] × 703
items.note: BMI is a population screening tool, not a diagnostic measure. It does not distinguish fat from muscle mass, does not capture fat distribution, and varies in accuracy by sex and age. WHO recommends waist circumference (>102 cm in men, >88 cm in women) as a complementary measure. For Asian populations, WHO identifies increased cardiometabolic risk starting at BMI 23.0.
items.source: WHO Global Classification (1997) · WHO Obesity Fact Sheet (2022) · WHO Expert Consultation on BMI for Asian Populations, The Lancet (2004) · NHLBI Clinical Guidelines

healthRisks.title: Why Your BMI Result Matters
healthRisks.intro: Awareness is the first step toward better health. Research from WHO and NHLBI consistently links sustained overweight (BMI ≥ 25) and obesity (BMI ≥ 30) to a higher likelihood of the following conditions. Recognising these connections early gives you the opportunity to take informed, preventive action.

overweightItems.desc: Obesity is a leading driver of heart disease worldwide. Reaching a healthy BMI reduces arterial inflammation, improves cholesterol levels, and significantly lowers the risk of heart attack and stroke.
Excess visceral fat is the primary modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes. Studies show that losing just 5–10% of body weight can delay or prevent onset in at-risk individuals.
Higher body mass directly increases the workload on the heart. Even modest, sustained weight loss produces clinically meaningful reductions in blood pressure.
WHO links obesity to at least 13 cancer types including breast, colorectal, and endometrial. Maintaining a healthy weight is one of the most evidence-backed forms of cancer prevention.
Each kilogram of excess weight places roughly four kilograms of additional stress on the knee joints. Achieving a healthy BMI reliably reduces joint pain, slows cartilage wear, and improves mobility.

healthRisks.underweightNote: A healthy BMI is a two-way target. Being underweight (BMI < 18.5) is equally important to address — it is associated with reduced bone density, weakened immunity, and nutritional deficiencies. Every step toward the 18.5–24.9 range is a step toward better health.

limitations.title: How to Use BMI Most Effectively
limitations.intro: BMI is a powerful starting point — not the complete picture. WHO and CDC both classify it as a screening tool, not a diagnostic one. Understanding where it works best helps you get the most value from your result:

items.title: Combine with waist circumference
items.desc: BMI does not show where fat is stored. Waist circumference (>102 cm in men, >88 cm in women) identifies visceral fat risk that BMI misses. Using both together gives a significantly stronger health signal than either measure alone.

items.title: Athletes and muscular individuals: add a body fat reading
items.desc: Muscle is denser than fat. If you train regularly, your BMI may read 'overweight' while your body fat is low. Pair your result with a body fat percentage measurement for an accurate picture of your composition.

items.title: Asian backgrounds: use WHO-adjusted thresholds
items.desc: A 2004 WHO Expert Consultation found that Asian populations face elevated cardiometabolic risk at lower BMI values. WHO recommends 23.0 as the 'increased risk' point and 27.5 as 'high risk' — apply these if relevant to you.

items.title: BMI is a screening start, not a medical verdict
items.desc: A BMI result alone cannot diagnose any condition. For a complete picture, your doctor will assess waist circumference, blood pressure, blood glucose, lipid panel, and lifestyle together with your BMI.

items.title: Track trends, not single readings
items.desc: A single BMI reading is a snapshot. Its real value emerges when tracked over time. Recalculate every 4–6 weeks when actively working on your health, and note the direction of change — that trend is what matters most.

faq.q: What are the official WHO BMI categories?
faq.a: WHO defines eight categories: Severe Thinness (< 16.0), Moderate Thinness (16.0–16.9), Mild Thinness (17.0–18.4), Normal weight (18.5–24.9), Pre-obesity (25.0–29.9), Obesity Class I (30.0–34.9), Obesity Class II (35.0–39.9), and Obesity Class III (≥ 40.0).

faq.q: What health risks are associated with high BMI?
faq.a: WHO and NHLBI data link overweight and obesity to type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, coronary heart disease, obstructive sleep apnoea, certain cancers, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. In 2021, higher-than-optimal BMI was associated with an estimated 3.7 million deaths globally.

faq.q: Is BMI accurate for athletes and muscular people?
faq.a: No. Muscle is denser than fat. An elite athlete or bodybuilder can register as 'overweight' or 'obese' while having very low body fat. Studies show BMI has only ~36% sensitivity for detecting actual obesity in men. Body fat percentage is a more accurate measure in such cases.

faq.q: Should Asian individuals use different BMI cutoffs?
faq.a: WHO retains the global standard thresholds (overweight ≥ 25, obesity ≥ 30). However, a 2004 WHO Expert Consultation published in The Lancet found Asian populations face increased cardiometabolic risk at lower BMI values — WHO identifies 23.0 as an 'increased risk' trigger and 27.5 as 'high risk' for Asian populations.

faq.q: Can children use this BMI calculator?
faq.a: No. This calculator is for adults aged 18 and above. Children and adolescents require age- and sex-specific BMI-for-age percentile charts because body composition changes significantly during growth. WHO and CDC publish separate BMI growth charts for children.

faq.q: Does BMI measure body fat directly?
faq.a: No. BMI is an indirect proxy based only on height and weight. Direct body fat measurement requires DEXA scan (gold standard), hydrostatic weighing, bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA), or skinfold caliper measurements.

faq.q: What should I do if my BMI is outside the normal range?
faq.a: BMI outside the healthy range is a signal for further assessment, not a diagnosis. Consult a doctor, registered dietitian, or certified fitness professional. They will evaluate waist circumference, blood pressure, blood glucose, lipid profile, and lifestyle before recommending any intervention.

metadata.title: BMR Calculator — Basal Metabolic Rate | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) using the Mifflin-St Jeor equation. Know exactly how many calories your body burns at rest.

items.title: BMR Calculator
items.tagline: How many calories does your body burn doing nothing?
items.shortDesc: Discover the calories your body burns at complete rest — the foundation of all calorie planning.
items.importance: Your BMR is the baseline for every nutrition and fitness plan. Whether you want to lose weight, gain muscle, or maintain your current body, understanding how many calories your body needs at rest allows you to make precise dietary adjustments and avoid under- or over-eating.
items.description: BMR is the number of calories your body needs to sustain basic life-sustaining functions at complete rest — breathing, circulation, cell production, and temperature regulation. It forms the foundation for all calorie planning.
items.note: W = weight in kg, H = height in cm, A = age in years. This is the most accurate equation for the general population.
items.source: Mifflin et al., 1990 — American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

faq.q: What does BMR mean in simple terms?
faq.a: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the number of calories your body burns just to stay alive — breathing, pumping blood, regulating temperature — even if you lie still all day and do nothing.

faq.q: Is it safe to eat below my BMR?
faq.a: No. Consistently eating below your BMR deprives your body of the energy it needs for vital organ functions. This can lead to muscle loss, nutritional deficiencies, fatigue, and metabolic slowdown. Never go below your BMR without medical supervision.

faq.q: Does BMR decrease with age?
faq.a: Yes. BMR typically decreases by 1–2% per decade after age 20, primarily because muscle mass (which is metabolically active) tends to decline with age. Resistance training can help slow this decline.

faq.q: How can I increase my BMR?
faq.a: Building lean muscle mass through strength training is the most effective way to raise BMR, as muscle tissue burns more calories at rest than fat. Staying well-hydrated, getting enough sleep, and eating adequate protein also help.

faq.q: Why is the Mifflin-St Jeor equation used?
faq.a: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation (1990) is considered the most accurate BMR formula for the general population in multiple validation studies. It outperforms older formulas like Harris-Benedict in accuracy for modern, diverse populations.

metadata.title: IBW Calculator — Ideal Body Weight | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate your Ideal Body Weight (IBW) using the Devine formula. Get a medically validated weight target based on your height and gender.

items.title: IBW Calculator
items.tagline: What is your medically recommended weight?
items.shortDesc: Find the medically recommended weight for your height using the Devine formula.
items.importance: Ideal Body Weight is used extensively in clinical medicine for calculating medication dosages, ventilator settings, and nutritional requirements. For fitness purposes, it provides a realistic weight target rooted in science rather than social trends or aesthetics.
items.description: Ideal Body Weight (IBW) is a clinical guideline for the weight considered healthy for a given height and gender. It is frequently used in medicine to calculate drug dosages, nutritional requirements, and fitness goals.
items.note: The result shows your IBW ± 10% as a healthy range. A 5-foot person is the baseline (60 inches).
items.source: Devine BJ, 1974 — Drug Intelligence & Clinical Pharmacy

faq.q: What is ideal body weight used for?
faq.a: IBW was originally developed for clinical use — calculating drug dosages, anaesthesia, and nutritional support in hospitals. For general fitness, it provides a science-based weight target that accounts for height and gender.

faq.q: Is IBW the same as my goal weight?
faq.a: Not necessarily. IBW is a medical guideline, not a personal fitness target. Your goal weight depends on your body composition, muscle mass, and individual health factors. Use IBW as a reference point, not an absolute target.

faq.q: Does IBW account for muscle mass or body composition?
faq.a: No. Like BMI, IBW is based only on height and gender. It does not distinguish between fat and muscle. A well-built, muscular person may weigh more than their IBW while still being very healthy.

faq.q: What if my weight is significantly below my IBW?
faq.a: Being significantly below your IBW may indicate underweight status or insufficient muscle mass, which can pose health risks. Consider consulting a doctor or dietitian if your weight is more than 10% below your IBW.

faq.q: Are there other IBW formulas besides Devine?
faq.a: Yes. The Hamwi formula (1964) and Robinson formula (1983) are alternatives. Each gives slightly different results. The Devine formula remains the most widely used in clinical settings due to its simplicity and validated use in pharmacology.

metadata.title: Body Fat Calculator — US Navy Method | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Estimate your body fat percentage using the US Navy circumference method. No equipment needed — just a tape measure.

items.title: Body Fat Calculator
items.tagline: What percentage of your body is fat?
items.shortDesc: Estimate your body fat percentage using simple measurements — no equipment needed.
items.importance: Body fat percentage is a far more meaningful measure of health and fitness than weight or BMI alone. Tracking it helps you understand whether you are losing fat vs. muscle, assess cardiovascular risk, and fine-tune your training and diet program for real body composition changes.
items.description: Body fat percentage is a more accurate measure of fitness than BMI because it directly quantifies the proportion of fat in your body. The US Navy method estimates this from simple circumference measurements without needing expensive equipment.
items.note: All measurements should be taken at the widest point. For best accuracy measure in the morning.
items.source: Hodgdon & Beckett, 1984 — Naval Health Research Center

faq.q: What is a healthy body fat percentage?
faq.a: For men, 6–13% is athlete-level, 14–17% is fitness, 18–24% is average, and above 25% is high. For women, 14–20% is athlete-level, 21–24% is fitness, 25–31% is average, and above 32% is high. Essential fat (needed for survival) is ~3–5% for men and ~10–13% for women.

faq.q: How accurate is the US Navy method?
faq.a: The Navy method has an error margin of approximately ±3–4% compared to DEXA scans (gold standard). It is suitable for general fitness tracking and progress monitoring but not for clinical precision. Consistent measurement technique improves reliability.

faq.q: What is essential body fat and why does it matter?
faq.a: Essential fat is the minimum amount of fat required for normal physiological function — hormone production, organ protection, and neurological function. For men it is ~3–5% and for women ~10–13%. Dropping below essential fat is dangerous and life-threatening.

faq.q: How often should I measure my body fat?
faq.a: Measure monthly for a meaningful trend. Daily measurements are unreliable because body fat readings fluctuate with hydration, food intake, and time of day. For best consistency, always measure at the same time of day, ideally in the morning before eating.

faq.q: Can I lose fat from specific body parts (spot reduction)?
faq.a: No. Spot reduction is a myth. Fat loss occurs systemically throughout the body based on genetics and hormones. Targeted exercises strengthen muscles in specific areas but do not burn fat from those areas specifically. A caloric deficit and overall exercise reduce fat everywhere.

metadata.title: TDEE Calculator — Total Daily Energy Expenditure | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE) to know exactly how many calories you need per day to lose, maintain, or gain weight.

items.title: TDEE Calculator
items.tagline: How many calories do you need each day to maintain your weight?
items.shortDesc: Calculate your total daily calorie burn based on your activity level to manage weight precisely.
items.importance: TDEE is the single most practical number for managing your weight. It tells you exactly how many calories you burn every day — at rest and through activity. Use it to create a precise calorie surplus for muscle gain, a deficit for fat loss, or maintenance for weight stability.
items.description: TDEE is your total calorie burn in a day, combining your BMR with your activity level. It is the most practical number for managing weight — eat below your TDEE to lose, above to gain, and at it to maintain.
items.formula:
TDEE = BMR × Activity Multiplier

Sedentary × 1.2 | Light × 1.375 | Moderate × 1.55
Active × 1.725  | Very Active × 1.9
items.note: A common strategy: subtract 500 kcal/day from TDEE to lose ~0.5 kg per week; add 500 kcal to gain.
items.source: Harris & Benedict, updated by Mifflin-St Jeor

faq.q: What is the difference between BMR and TDEE?
faq.a: BMR is the calories your body burns at complete rest. TDEE adds the calories burned through all daily activities — exercise, walking, work, and even digestion. TDEE is always higher than BMR and is the number you should use for diet planning.

faq.q: How do I use TDEE to lose weight?
faq.a: Eat 300–500 kcal below your TDEE daily for safe, sustainable fat loss of approximately 0.3–0.5 kg per week. Avoid deficits larger than 750–1000 kcal/day as this risks muscle loss, nutrient deficiency, and metabolic adaptation.

faq.q: How accurate is the TDEE calculation?
faq.a: TDEE estimates are typically within ±10–15% accuracy. Activity multipliers are averages and don't capture individual variation. The best approach: use the calculated TDEE as a starting point, track your weight for 2–3 weeks, and adjust intake by 100–200 kcal if needed.

faq.q: What happens if I consistently eat at my TDEE?
faq.a: If you eat exactly at your TDEE, your weight should remain stable over time. This is called maintenance eating. It is a good strategy during phases of muscle building (recomposition) or simply maintaining a healthy weight after reaching your goal.

faq.q: Does TDEE change as I lose weight or gain muscle?
faq.a: Yes. As you lose weight, your TDEE decreases because a lighter body requires fewer calories. As you gain muscle, TDEE increases slightly. Recalculate your TDEE every 4–6 weeks or whenever your weight changes by more than 2–3 kg.
